coding_rate_limiter_001: Broken assumption: tenant_independent (OBJECTIVE). The token/leaky/window family enforces a per-tenant ceiling and is blind to whether tenants share a bottleneck; it cannot deliver coupled max-min fairness. Reframe the objective: the limiter does not cap each tenant at a static rate but solves, per shared bottleneck, a max-min water-filling that admits a tenant only up to its bottleneck-fair share, recomputed as the active-tenant set changes. New output: a per-(tenant,bottleneck) dynamic fair-share signal, not a per-tenant rate constant. New data required: production_traces giving which tenants actually collide on which downstream resource (a static config cannot supply this). Why families can't: token_bucket/GCRA/sliding_window give an isolated guarantee, so an idle tenant's unused share is wasted while a busy one is starved at its cap. Branches in tension: (conservative) couple objective to max-min; (radical) measure share over the bottleneck resource not over time; (hybrid) weight share by request cost. Chosen: max-min coupling. Theorem sketch: with N active tenants on a bottleneck of capacity C, the scheme converges to the max-min allocation and is work-conserving (no idle capacity while any tenant is throttled). RED-FIRST test: test_objective_maxmin_reclaims_idle_share in tests/test_fairshare.py. Baseline lacks it: feed 3 tenants where tenant A is idle; assert busy B,C each get C/2, not their static cap C/3 -> token-bucket baseline fails (leaves C/3 idle). Negative control: shuffle the tenant->bottleneck mapping so collisions are random; the reclaim must vanish (proves it tracks real coupling, not noise). Command: pytest -k test_objective_maxmin_reclaims_idle_share. Max claim allowed: strictly more work-conserving under shared bottlenecks than per-tenant limiters on the production trace; NOT a universal replacement.
coding_cache_002: Broken assumption: cost_uniform / time_windowed -> here I break REPRESENTATION: fairness is measured over time-windowed request counts. A bursty tenant evicting another's hot set is a fairness failure that a request-rate limiter cannot see, because the scarce resource is cache residency (byte-seconds), not requests. Reframe: the caching layer enforces fair-share over an occupancy resource — each tenant gets a max-min share of byte-seconds of residency, and eviction is driven by which tenant is over its current fair occupancy share, not by global LRU. New output: per-tenant occupancy-debt signal feeding a fair eviction victim selector. New data: production_traces of per-tenant key reuse distributions (workload_distribution) to set warm-set sizes. Why families can't: LRU/LFU/window limiters are tenant-blind; a burst tenant with one-shot keys evicts a steady tenant's reused warm set and global hit-rate looks fine while the victim's hit-rate collapses. Branches: (conservative) per-tenant LRU partitions (rejected: wastes capacity, not work-conserving); (radical) measure over byte-seconds residency; (hybrid) cost-weight by miss penalty. Chosen: occupancy max-min with work-conserving borrowing (idle tenants lend capacity, reclaimed on demand). RED-FIRST test: test_representation_burst_protects_warmset in tests/test_faircache.py. Baseline (global LRU) fails: tenant A streams unique keys, tenant B has a small reused warm set; assert B's hit-rate stays >= 0.9 under A's burst -> LRU drops it near A's churn rate. Negative control: randomize which tenant owns the warm set each request so there is no stable warm set; protection must disappear. Command: pytest -k test_representation_burst_protects_warmset. Max claim: protects warm-set hit-rate of victim tenants under bursts better than global LRU at equal total capacity; NOT higher aggregate hit-rate in the uncontended case.
coding_backpressure_003: Broken assumption: cost_uniform (COST_MODEL). Standard backpressure (bounded queues, AQM/CoDel, token shedding) counts items, so under heterogeneous request cost a tenant sending a few very expensive requests passes the count-based gate while monopolizing service; correctness of every count-based policy breaks. Reframe: backpressure is applied on an estimated-work budget — admission and shedding are governed by each tenant's outstanding-work fair share (sum of per-request cost estimates in flight), with cost estimated online and corrected by measured service time (a virtual-finish-time / cost-fair-queueing signal). New output: per-tenant in-flight-work debt and a shed/admit decision keyed on it, not on queue length. New data: production_traces to fit the cost estimator and its error distribution. Why families can't: bounded-queue + count fairness equalize item rate, so a tenant with 10x-cost items gets 10x the server while staying within its item quota. Branches: (conservative) couple to max-min over work; (radical) measure work-units not time; (hybrid) both. Chosen: cost-aware fair backpressure. Theorem sketch: under bounded estimator error e, the scheme bounds the service-time share gap between any two backlogged tenants by O(e) and stays work-conserving. RED-FIRST test: test_costmodel_expensive_tenant_throttled in tests/test_backpressure.py. Baseline (count-based) fails: tenant A sends cost-10 jobs, B sends cost-1 jobs at equal item rate; assert measured service-time share of A <= 0.55 -> count-based gives A ~0.9. Negative control: set all costs equal (homogeneous); the new scheme and the baseline must converge to the same shares (proves the win comes from cost heterogeneity, not the mechanism). Command: pytest -k test_costmodel_expensive_tenant_throttled. Max claim: bounds service-time unfairness under cost heterogeneity that count-based backpressure cannot; gains vanish when costs are uniform.
coding_scheduler_004: Broken assumption: time_windowed (REPRESENTATION). Throttling by request count over a window controls arrival rate but not the resource that actually saturates a gateway — in-flight concurrency / hold time. A few slow upstream calls saturate connection pools while the request-rate gate reads green. Reframe: the scheduler measures and limits the integral of concurrency (Little's law: occupancy = arrival x latency), allocating each tenant a max-min share of a concurrency budget and admitting only while the tenant is under its current fair in-flight share; the budget self-tunes to keep measured latency at an SLO (a concurrency-limit / gradient-controller view, but fair-shared per tenant). New output: per-tenant fair concurrency limit that floats with observed latency, not a fixed RPS. New data: workload_distribution / traces of per-tenant service-time tails. Why count-families can't: RPS limits set with average latency collapse when a tenant's latency spikes — same request rate, exploding occupancy, pool exhaustion. Branches: (conservative) couple concurrency share max-min across tenants; (radical) drop time entirely, schedule on occupancy; (hybrid) cost-weight concurrency. Chosen: fair adaptive concurrency. Theorem sketch: if the controller holds total in-flight near L*, p99 latency stays bounded under latency shocks where any fixed-RPS limit would not. RED-FIRST test: test_representation_concurrency_caps_under_latency_spike in tests/test_scheduler.py. Baseline (RPS limiter) fails: hold request rate fixed, 5x the upstream latency; assert in-flight <= budget and p99 bounded -> RPS limiter lets in-flight blow past the pool. Negative control: hold latency constant; concurrency and RPS limiting become equivalent (no advantage), proving the win is the latency-coupling. Command: pytest -k test_representation_concurrency_caps_under_latency_spike. Max claim: bounds occupancy/p99 under latency shocks where request-count throttling cannot; equivalent to RPS limiting when latency is stationary.
math_convergence_001: Broken assumption: smoothness_required (HYPOTHESIS). L-smooth GD/SGD theory needs a global Lipschitz gradient, which fails on many non-convex objectives (deep nets, phase retrieval, Poisson likelihoods) whose curvature grows with the iterate. Weaken the hypothesis to relative smoothness w.r.t. a reference Bregman kernel h plus a KL (Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz) inequality, and prove convergence of a Bregman/NoLips-type step with that kernel rather than a Euclidean step. New output: a curvature-adaptive stepsize tied to the Bregman geometry and a rate stated in the Bregman divergence, with KL giving iterate (not just gradient-norm) convergence to a critical point. New data: curvature_info — a verified relative-smoothness constant L_h, since the ceiling cannot move without it. Why families can't: GD/Adam/Newton either diverge (no global L) or need full Hessians; relative smoothness needs only the kernel. Branches: (conservative) relative-smoothness+KL; (radical) distrust gradient on rugged regions; (hybrid) non-Euclidean metric. Chosen: relative-smoothness + KL. Theorem sketch: if f is L_h-relatively-smooth w.r.t. h and satisfies KL with exponent theta, Bregman descent with step <= 1/L_h yields min_k D_h-gradient -> 0 at O(1/k) and, under KL(theta), finite-length iterates converging to a critical point with rate set by theta. RED-FIRST test: test_hypothesis_relsmooth_converges_without_L in tests/test_relsmooth.py. Baseline lacks it: build f with unbounded Hessian (e.g. quartic) where fixed-step GD diverges; assert Bregman-step gradient-surrogate -> 0 below tol. Negative control: replace the matched kernel h by the Euclidean kernel (h = ||.||^2/2); the iterate must diverge, proving the kernel — not the step — earns convergence. Command: pytest -k test_hypothesis_relsmooth_converges_without_L. Max claim: convergence on a relatively-smooth+KL class where no global-L method has a guarantee; NOT a faster rate on standard L-smooth problems.
math_geometry_002: Broken assumption: euclidean_metric (STRUCTURE) together with smoothness_required. The complexity of GD/Adam is governed by L and the Euclidean condition number, which is an artifact of the chosen metric, not of the problem. Make the rate depend on an intrinsic geometric quantity — relative smoothness/strong-convexity (L_h, mu_h) measured against a problem-matched Bregman kernel h, equivalently the condition number in the Hessian/Riemannian metric — so the rate is the relative condition number L_h/mu_h, invariant to Euclidean rescaling. New output: a rate certificate stated in the intrinsic (mirror/Riemannian) geometry, plus the kernel that realizes it. New data: curvature_info to construct h (the reference convex function whose Bregman divergence dominates f). Why families can't: GD's rate is metric-dependent and explodes on ill-conditioned-in-Euclidean but well-conditioned-in-h problems; Newton fixes it only with full Hessians. Branches: (conservative) relative-smoothness rate; (radical) gradient untrusted, use geometry-aware oracle; (hybrid) Riemannian. Chosen: intrinsic-geometry rate. Theorem sketch: for f that is (mu_h, L_h)-relatively-strongly-convex/smooth w.r.t. h, mirror descent in h contracts the Bregman divergence at rate (1 - mu_h/L_h), independent of the Euclidean condition number. RED-FIRST test: test_structure_rate_invariant_to_conditioning in tests/test_geometry.py. Baseline (Euclidean GD) fails: take a family of problems with fixed relative condition number but Euclidean condition number scaled 1..1000; assert iterations-to-tol stays flat for the mirror method -> GD's iteration count grows ~linearly with the Euclidean number. Negative control: use a mismatched kernel (Euclidean h); the flatness must vanish, proving the geometry drives the rate. Command: pytest -k test_structure_rate_invariant_to_conditioning. Max claim: a rate invariant to Euclidean rescaling, strictly better than L-based bounds on problems ill-conditioned only in the Euclidean metric; NOT universally faster.
math_proximal_003: Broken assumption: smoothness_required (HYPOTHESIS) for the proximal/forward-backward family. Standard proximal-gradient needs the smooth part to be L-smooth to bound the forward step; replace that with relative smoothness w.r.t. a Legendre kernel h and prove a Bregman proximal-gradient (Bregman forward-backward / NoLips with a nonsmooth term) convergence theorem. New output: a Bregman-prox iteration x_{k+1}=argmin{ g(x)+<grad f(x_k),x>+ (1/t) D_h(x,x_k) } with a rate in D_h and, under a Bregman-PL/KL condition, linear convergence — none of which requires a global L. New data: curvature_info giving the relative-smoothness constant L_h so the step t<=1/L_h is certified. Why families can't: ISTA/FISTA/Euclidean prox set step <=1/L and diverge or stall when f has no global L (e.g. KL-divergence data terms, Poisson inverse problems); Newton-prox needs Hessians. Branches: (conservative) relative-smoothness prox; (radical) distrust gradient; (hybrid) Riemannian prox. Chosen: Bregman proximal-gradient under relative smoothness. Theorem sketch: if f is L_h-relatively-smooth w.r.t. Legendre h and g is closed convex with tractable Bregman-prox, then for t<=1/L_h the iterates satisfy F(x_k)-F* <= D_h(x*,x_0)/(t k) (O(1/k)); adding a Bregman-PL inequality yields linear rate (1 - t mu). RED-FIRST test: test_hypothesis_bregman_prox_converges_no_globalL in tests/test_bregprox.py. Baseline (Euclidean ISTA) fails: F = Poisson/KL data term (no global L) + L1; assert Bregman-prox objective gap -> 0 at the predicted 1/k slope. Negative control: swap Legendre h for the Euclidean kernel; the iteration must blow up or stall, proving the kernel earns the guarantee. Command: pytest -k test_hypothesis_bregman_prox_converges_no_globalL. Max claim: O(1/k) (linear under Bregman-PL) for composite problems with no global L where Euclidean prox has no guarantee; NOT an improvement on L-smooth composite problems.
generic_unknown_001: No matched pack, so I elicit the domain rather than answer from the average. (1) Assumptions every matchmaking system shares: skill is one scalar (Elo/Glicko/TrueSkill); the objective is balance = equal win-probability; matches are pairwise/team symmetric; quality is judged at match-formation time; queue wait and balance are independent knobs; the population is stationary. (2) Breakable axes: OBJECTIVE (balance vs experienced enjoyment/retention), REPRESENTATION (scalar skill vs multi-dim playstyle + uncertainty), MEASURE (predicted win-prob vs measured post-match churn/blowout), DECOMPOSITION (form-then-play vs form-conditioned-on-predicted-experience), EVALUATION (offline rating fit vs A/B retention). (3) Families to collide against: Elo, Glicko-2, TrueSkill/TrueSkill2, OpenSkill, and rating+queue-time heuristics. (4) New information to move the ceiling: per-player post-match enjoyment/continuation signal (did they queue again, rage-quit, report a blowout) — an observable today's systems never optimize. Recommended break: OBJECTIVE — stop optimizing predicted win-probability balance and optimize expected experienced fairness (low blowout probability AND retention) measured after the match, since a 50/50 predicted match can still be a stomp. World-test where balance-family fails and the reframe wins: a cohort where 50% win-prob matches still produce frequent blowouts (high score variance); optimizing measured non-blowout rate beats optimizing predicted balance. Counterexample that kills it: if measured blowout rate is uncorrelated with anything the matcher controls, the new objective is unsteerable. RED-FIRST test: test_objective_minimizes_blowout_not_winprob in tests/test_matchmaking.py — generate players with a hidden variance trait so equal-Elo matches still blow out; assert the new matcher's blowout rate < the Elo-balanced matcher's at equal mean win-prob. Negative control: zero the variance trait (pure-scalar world); both matchers must tie, proving the win comes from the second skill dimension, not the optimizer. Command: pytest -k test_objective_minimizes_blowout_not_winprob. Max claim: lower blowout/quit rate at equal predicted balance when a hidden variance dimension exists; NOT better when skill truly is one scalar. Data-insufficient flag: needs the post-match experience signal — a new combination of rating systems cannot raise the ceiling without it.
self_improve_001: No pack matched; I treat OUTLIER_MCB's own evaluation as the domain and break its core assumption. Broken assumption (OBJECTIVE + MEASURE): the engine scores an idea by internal structure — box_distance x survives_falsification — which rewards being far from the average answer, not being valuable. An idea can be maximally structure-novel and useless; the proxy and the target diverge. Reframe: measure realized value — does the proposed red-first test, when actually executed in the target repo, FLIP from red to green only for the broken-assumption idea and stay red for the negative control, and does that flip correlate with an external outcome (benchmark delta, accepted PR, downstream metric). New output: a value certificate = (test went red->green) AND (negative control stayed red) AND (a registered external metric improved), replacing the self-referential novelty score. New data: an outcome ledger linking each shipped idea to a measured real result — the observable the engine currently lacks. Why current design can't: novelty x falsifiability is computable without ever running anything in the world, so it cannot distinguish 'survives the engine's own test' from 'helps a real user.' Branches: (conservative) keep novelty, add an executed-test gate; (radical) replace the objective with measured outcome only; (hybrid) novelty as a tie-breaker among value-positive ideas. Chosen: hybrid — value-gated, novelty-ranked. World-test where the structure-objective fails and value wins: a corpus of ideas with high box_distance but whose tests never flip or whose flips don't move any metric; ranking by realized value de-ranks them while the old score promotes them. RED-FIRST test: test_objective_ranks_by_realized_value_not_structure in tests/test_value.py — feed two ideas, A (high box_distance, test never flips / control also flips) and B (modest distance, test flips and control stays red); assert the engine ranks B above A. The current scorer ranks A first -> red today. Negative control: make B's negative control ALSO flip (so its 'value' is noise); B must lose its rank, proving the gate measures faithful value, not just any test flip. Command: pytest -k test_objective_ranks_by_realized_value_not_structure. Max claim: realized-value ranking suppresses structure-novel-but-useless ideas that the current score promotes; NOT a claim that novelty is irrelevant. Data-insufficient flag: needs the outcome ledger — internal scores alone cannot certify value.
routing_ambiguous_001: The request 'a fast convergence algorithm with low latency' is genuinely ambiguous: 'convergence' routes to the optimization pack while 'low latency' routes to the rate-limiter/systems pack, and the two are near-tied. I will not fake a single answer; I disambiguate, because the broken assumption is different in each reading and a blended answer would be INSIDE_THE_BOX for both. Reading A (optimization): you want an iterative method that reaches tolerance in fewer/cheaper iterations — then the live assumption to break is smoothness_required/euclidean_metric, and the right move is a relative-smoothness or intrinsic-geometry rate (see the geometry/relative-smoothness designs), with world-test test_structure_rate_invariant_to_conditioning. Reading B (systems): you want a request scheduler/limiter that holds tail latency low under load — then the assumption to break is time_windowed, and the move is throttling by concurrency/occupancy (Little's law) rather than request count, with world-test test_representation_concurrency_caps_under_latency_spike. Reading C (distributed consensus): 'fast convergence + low latency' could mean a low-latency agreement protocol, where the assumption to break is the round-based representation. These break different axes (STRUCTURE vs REPRESENTATION), so committing to one without confirmation risks answering the wrong problem. Which is it: an optimizer's iteration complexity, a gateway's p99 under load, or a consensus/gossip convergence time? RED-FIRST contract is whichever you pick — for A: pytest -k test_structure_rate_invariant_to_conditioning; for B: pytest -k test_representation_concurrency_caps_under_latency_spike, each with the negative control named above. Max claim allowed: none until the domain is fixed — naming the wrong box is the failure mode here, so the honest output is the disambiguating question plus the two ready red-first tests, not a merged 'fast low-latency algorithm' that satisfies neither world-test.
